speaker: interviewee
text: When I worked in the depot already, from this ghetto out, I worked-- we were cleaning the smiths-- what are they called-- all the places, the workplaces for the people that worked over there. And we also washed the clothes, the uniforms, fatigues from the people that did the locomotive, fixing locomotives, fixing the cars, train cars and stuff like that. So we washed these clothes for them. And a lot of people worked over there, boys, who worked carpentry-- carpentry. And girls, some girls worked in the office. And some girls were cleaning their offices. We were not cleaning the offices. We worked over there. There were a lot of rumors. Every day came out another rumor. One time, when we worked for the smith, a Polish man came over. And I worked there. And there was a boy, who was named Zimering. His name was Zimering, a boy also from Kowel. I didn't know him.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we both worked there, cleaning, doing. He helped them work whatever. And I was cleaning. And he said to us that tonight, they're going to take out everybody from the ghetto, the Jews, and kill them. If you want to come sleep over, stay overnight by me, you could do it. So you could stay alive. So if he says this, we both decided we'll go there. So we had to cross the railroad tracks and things to come to his house. He was nice. He gave us a-- not a mattress, some straw or something on the floor. We slept over the night. But thank God, nothing happened that night. We came straight back to work.

speaker: interviewee
text: Another time there were rumors, same thing, they going to do that. One time, they said that that's what's going to happen this night. So I-- I figured, I'm not going home. I'm not going to the ghetto. I went out. And also, a Polish man told me, if any time I need to go to his house, I could always be there by him. So I figured, OK. This night, that's what they said. I'll go there. I come there. And I look, and I look. And I couldn't find-- I couldn't find the number he gave me, the address he gave me. I saw a house. There was a building, and it wasn't finished. I thought-- it was already late, and it was dark, so I went in there. And there was nothing there. There was straw. But I saw a sheet metal, a big sheet metal sheet. I lay down. I covered myself with the sheet metal. And I slept over the night. I went back to work tomorrow-- next day. I came back to work. OK, thank God nothing happened in the ghetto. That's it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So then it was go on. We was-- we were working. And all of the sudden, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] comes a-- a order that who's going to have a schein, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] your working schein, is going to stay in this ghetto. And who won't-- again, the same thing-- has to go to the other ghetto. So we all-- one person that was cleaning the office, an old officer, an old man, a German, had a schein. And all of us didn't have any scheins. We went into the office, and we started crying. We're working, and how come we don't have the schein? We don't-- we didn't get any working schein. So he said to us, tomorrow, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I'm going to find out whether it's true what you're saying. Because we told them everything what was going on.

speaker: interviewee
text: If it's true what you are saying, and I'm going to find out-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] he had a secretary, a German young man, rather young, probably a 25- or 26-years-old. He said, if this is true what you are saying, all of you, four o'clock, you should all be at the Judenrat. And I'll send Neumann to take out a night shift, so he'll put you all in order. And you'll get out of the ghetto for the night.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was Neumann?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's him, the young man, the German--

speaker: interviewer
text: Ah.

speaker: interviewee
text: --German secretary--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: --what worked for that old man.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the old man's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: This, I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because the reason I remember him, because that's what he said to us. The girl that worked for him, she probably knew his name. So we-- nobody wants to go to that other ghetto that was for the next day. Nobody wants to go to the other ghetto. I had a friend, Landsberg. He was a friend from home and our next door neighbor. And he worked for Kassner. Kassner was the murderer that-- that liquidated the ghetto in Rowne and next was Kowel.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he worked for Kassner. And he [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] asked him, asked him directly, because he worked for him, made him boots. He was a shoemaker. He made for him boots. He made shoes for his family to send to Germany. He did--

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, no, nothing is going to happen. Nothing is going to happen. He told her sister to go there. Because if she would be-- he said, if she would be in the ghetto, where she is now, she's going to be killed. So his sister, the only sister he had.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were walking, walking around, just by the Judenrat, all of us. It was about-- and we were about 16 people working there, husband and wife and on. So we were walking around about 4 o'clock. We see Neumann is coming.

speaker: interviewee
text: He went into the-- to the Judenrat. And he took-- gave them the order. And he said, I have my people. And he stood us all up. And he took us out of the ghetto to the depot. Now everybody went to their place. We were six girls on the roof, on the attic of that office, where the old man was. There were a few boys. I don't know how many boys, five or six boys, and they carve. They-- they're working carpentry. And there was a wife and a husband in the office where they worked. It was 16 people, all together. And about-- I can-- don't know the time, but it was-- it must have been in the morning, early morning. That old man opened that door from the attic, the German, and said, his eyes should never in his life see what he saw. He probably wanted to convince himself. So he went there. So he say, he should never in his life see things he saw. And we stayed there for three days on that attic. And they send-- brought us up food. I think that Neumann did not. The old man used to give us up the food through that door. And when we got out, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there was already over-- all the people were killed, over there, in that ghetto. And the same-- the same thing can how. The people that were left, hidden over there, someplace, came back to this ghetto. And this is when he found out his sister wasn't there anymore. And his-- and whoever was in that ghetto did not come back anymore. My family was there, because they had the scheins, working scheins. So they were still there, my uncle and aunt and the cousins.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when we got out, I came into the places that I worked. The murderers ask me, how come you are here? How are you alive?

speaker: interviewee
text: I says, well, I had a schein. I did not. Because then, later, they did give scheins to us. After this already, a few days later, we got scheins to work. He said, I had-- we had working scheins. Because they were Germans that are working. They're with Polish people, together.

speaker: interviewee
text: So then we started to think how-- how to get out and what to do. So I had a friend that I went to school with, which his name was Prestupa. And he lived in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he told me, a little-- a few day-- a few weeks later, he told me that it's something very bad. And that he would love to take me there where he works. At that time, he worked already in a-- in the forest. He was an accountant. He worked in a forest. And he said that he has already five or six people there. And he's afraid that it'll be something suspicious.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he will ask a woman, his friend, that she-- she-- he already talked to her. And she'll take me to a village where I could survive. I could work for them, for the people in the village.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is the way I got out of the ghetto. By that time already, people were working. The Warsaw boys, from Warsaw, made passport. They redid my passport from a Ukrainian passport-- a Russian passport to a Ukrainian passport. And I didn't have already my Jewish name. I already had Katya and Sekeluk instead of Sekel. So and this is when I had this passport. And this woman came. She did not. She send her daughter, with a baby, one day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was the woman?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was-- the daughter's married name, I don't know. Her maiden name was Lugosz. Her father's name was Jan Lugosz. And I don't-- don't remember exactly, was it Maria, her mother's name? I really don't remember. Because I didn't see her too many times. I saw her just once over there. And-- because he said, this is the woman that's going to take you to that-- to the village. And-- but she figured, if her daughter comes, and she's young, and she'll give-- she gave me the baby, to carry the baby through the gate. And I passed through the gate with the baby.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have Polish clothes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. I had-- these people, my friends, Landsberg, he worked there in the-- in the school where they sorted out the Jewish-- the clothes that they killed the ghettos out-- the Jews. They used to take the clothes, the gold, just everything there, what they had. And they took people to sort it out. And by sorting out, they used to, every day, when we went home-- I worked there, too, for a few days. When we were home, they-- he would look. The Kassner was looking, from the top down, with bi-- binoculars, and looking what's going on, what they doing. If somebody would put food-- they didn't put anything else. If there were potatoes or were their bread, they would put in the packet to take it home to the ghetto. They hung them. Every day, they were hanging people for nothing, for a potato putting in the pocket, for a piece of bread putting in a pocket. He-- I don't know how. Maybe he put it on himself. I really don't know how he did it. He worked, actually, not outside sorting it. They worked in the shoemaker's thing. He brought me a pair of shoes, with-- well, with laces, like the girls go to church with, and-- and a blouse, an embroidered Ukrainian blouse. And this is what I put on and went out from the ghetto with her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did she take you?

speaker: interviewee
text: She took me to her house in Kowel. But I was not supposed to tell. Her father is not supposed to know that I am Jewish. Her father knew that I am Polish. And I don't want to go to the Russian-- to the Germans to work in Germany. So I want to go someplace, work here, where somebody's allowed to have people work. Because he was very scared. I guess he had trouble. His mother, his wife, what it was she was or she wasn't, but she had trouble with the police in Poland about communism and stuff like that. So he was very scared of police. So I wasn't supposed to tell him that. And she took me to Kleczkowicze, a village, where I stayed with a couple. They had a small child. And they were allowed to have one person working for them, a paying person.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I stayed them for-- with them. I was-- they were in the harvest season. It must have been August. In August, they were-- were. Too, I was working in the fields. I was working in the garden, with tomatoes, with whatever they had to do. I did whatever they did.

speaker: interviewee
text: And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] all of a sudden-- and she used to come in, once a week, and give me the report and told me that they already liquidated this ghetto. And a lot Jews were killed where they were running away. They didn't want to go the ghetto. So they run around a little. They-- they shoot them, whoever they caught. And they-- it-- it was horrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: My family, I lost there too, no uncle, no aunt, no nobody. So I-- I knew that. But she did not want that anybody should know. So she came out to the garden, like taking a tomato or whatever she wanted to take. And she told me all this news. All of a sudden, one day, one night, some-- I did not mingle with the young kids in that village. I was scared, so I didn't go out. They used to go out in the evening and sit on the-- on the logs. They were logs laying there and sit on the log, and singing, whatever. I didn't go.

speaker: interviewee
text: So one night, they knocked at the door, said, Jew, come out. And this-- this woman-- both of them were very scared. They were very scared. And I said to her, I'm not Jewish. You don't have to be worried about it. I'm not.

speaker: interviewee
text: She says, I know. But if they say it, if they want, they're going to kill you. So because they did. While I was there, they were running around, Ukrainians, in the potatoes, the potatoes, in the fields from other, like corn or whatever, looking where Jews were hiding. If one run away, hiding someplace in the field, they were finding him and killing him with their own hands, with their own--

speaker: interviewee
text: So she was very scared and very afraid. So-- so was I. So she told me, maybe you go to my sister. I have a sister in this and this village. So this is when I decided, I first have to go to Maciejow and try to see to get another piece of document to my passport.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because my passport, really, if I would be unlucky and they'd take it to the light, they would see it was worked with. I mean, those kids were not professionals that they did. They did the best they could and with the best chemicals they-- they had.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went to Maciejow then. And that was the day. On the way when I walked-- it was on a Sunday. I walked there. And they were bringing, from the villages, Jews on the-- on the buggies, horse and buggies thing. So they were-- and some of them said to me, come on, sit down here. You'll be-- why do you have to walk, you're going? They-- I said no, I'd rather walk than sit down.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went over to Maciejow. And the first thing there was the hospital. There was a hospital at the end of the city. And I walked in this hospital. And the hospital was [SOBBING] just horrible. It was just horrible scenes. Some doctors and nurses committed suicide. Because they knew that, tonight, they going to take them out and kill them, probably, whatever they knew.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they committed suicide. And it was such a mess in that hospital. But I did see a doctor. I did see a doctor. I suppose I was real sick already, too, inside. So he gave me a prescription. And I already had a piece of paper, at least, with a name on it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- and then it got dark. Because until they found a doctor for me, because the Jewish doctors didn't work already. They were not supposed to work already. But they were in the hospital to do something for themselves, whatever.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went into a house that was right across the street from the hospital. And I asked the people what I could sleep over the night. Because it's dark, and I'm from a village, and I don't want work at night-- walk at night. So they said to me, we would be very happy to accommodate you. We have enough room. But why should you be a-- suffer because of us? They say that they coming out at night. That we have to-- they'll take us out from the houses. And we don't know where they going to take us. And you're not Jewish. Why do you have to suffer? I mean, they'll take you because you are here. Oh, and this was a Jewish house.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was a Jewish house, right. So he said, they-- they not going, you know-- while you going to be here, you're going to take her with us. They going to take you with us. So I said, thank you, and I walked out. And I didn't know what to do. I just didn't know what to do. I didn't go to my cousin. I had a cousin over there. But I didn't go. Already, it was late at night. And I didn't remember, because I was a young-- I was very young when he got married.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I go back. I figured, OK, I'll walk at night. I'll walk there. I'll walk back home from where I came from, to that family. So I see a small-- a house. The first house I saw that would be big, I would go into. So I walked in.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I asked-- there was an old man that was the boss, actually. And I asked him that I could sleep over the night. There was a real little, tiny house. And there was a husband and a wife and a child and one son that was probably sickly, and that old man. He said, but we don't have any room in here. So you have to sleep-- you'll have to go to the barn to sleep. I said, OK, I could sleep at the barn. So I went to the barn. I went to the barn to sleep. I went up on a ladder. They told me to go up where the hay is. I went up, and I tried to lay down and sleep. And then I heard somebody coming into the barn. So he send in his-- that older son, there, probably to be downstairs in the barn, to see if somebody comes in to bother me or whatever, so like a protection or something.

speaker: interviewee
text: At first, I was very scared. Because I thought, who knows? I mean, you never know. But I never saw him. I just know that he was there. But I never saw him. I mean, I saw him in the morning, in the house, when we ate breakfast. Did you get the paper for your passport in Maciejow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I got-- this is-- was-- first, I asked him, am I too slow? I asked him to go back home to get something. He said, OK. I mean, I didn't want to just--

speaker: interviewee
text: But then, when I came back to the place, to get my underwear or something, I had nothing. So my-- the lady said, my God, why did you come? They're looking for you all over. And she didn't let me take anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because if you're going to carry a package, it's not good. So I went right back. I went right back, and I came back. And I worked for the man, maybe, a few days, five days or something. He took some fish that he raised. He went to the city hall and gave me another document, made a -- a document in the same name, that I'm working for him, with the name and everything, that he needs me.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this is the way I had a next document, and that I am Ukrainian with this. Now they gave out orders that nobody could have anybody working, because they need workers in Germany. You could only work for German people. So this old man says to me, there is a big farm right here, next-- just a few-- half a block. And you'll go there. And they're going to hire you. And I went there. And they hired me. And that was Stachor, it was called.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I worked for the-- it was-- the administrator was a Polish man. But it was for the Germans. Everything went for the Germans. So I worked over there until the Germans started to go back. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do for them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did everything. I was-- actually, I-- I-- I took care of the-- the milk that was milked from the cows, how much milk it came in. And we took it to the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to the milk place that they processed this.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we had big cans. And I had to measure everything, from every cow, what come out, and what we bring it over there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one time, I go there, because I had to bring it. And I went there. And a policeman comes right across from me. And there was a man that went to school with me. So since then, every night, when I was sleeping and something came in, some noise or something, I thought that he is coming. I was-- but probably, he did not notice me, that we passed by, I with my buggy and he with his buggy, that way-- this way. And he must have not noticed me. Because, otherwise, I don't know whether I would be alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: So--

speaker: interviewee
text: But I lived already in-- in-- you know, every night, anything it was I heard, I thought that's him or the police coming for me. So-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] but I was in Maciejow until-- until the-- the partisans started to fight. The Ukrainian partisans--

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: --came in. It was in 1943. 1943, the partisans, they were coming to the-- the Russian partisans come in, in one village. And they-- when they leave, the other ones-- there was Banderovcis, and Bulba. That was Ukrainian leaders from years ago. And they were-- they were fighting with each other, with the Poles. They were killing Polish people living there. They were-- kill everything. So when I-- when-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when I was scared to be in that town, in Maciejow, I was very scared.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the girl that worked with me on that-- there was a-- a woman, with her husband and child. They were working on the German farm with me. So she said, if you come to our village, there is no such a thing, like, you know, like this. Come over. You'll stay with us, with my-- with my father and mother and us. She had a family over there. So when I was here, I was-- and-- and she was already home with her family. And I saw that it was very bad that if I-- if I was this, it's not good if these partisans come. If I'm this, it's not good when these come. So I went over there. I went to her. And when I walked, I-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was-- it was very scary. And I came there-- What-- where was it, the village?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nowosiolki, it was in Nowosiolki that they lived. I went there. And I-- probably it was called Nowosiolki, because the Polish people settled over there. It's mean that there are new settlers.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I-- I came there. And I was with them. And all of a sudden, we had to leave. Because they said that the-- the Ukrainian partisans are coming in that night.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russians just used to come and go, because they had orders to go in the back of the Germans to do some sabotage or whatever.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she was afraid to stay there and said to me, you could go with us if you want to, if you go to the Polish partisans. So we went there. I went with her. And we traveled all night to go to Luboml.

speaker: interviewee
text: We traveled with gypsies. There were a lot of gypsies. And it was very scary, because they made a lot of noise. But they were nice. They were nice, nice people. We lived through the night and went in Luboml to a-- to a priest. And she got all the orders where to go, because the priest knew already where that is. So we went through with a train to the Polish partisans. And I came to the Polish partisans maybe two days. I walked there. We went through the dining room, whatever we went. I took a look. I saw a man, a young man that also worked on the farm. But he knew that I was Ukrainian. So I was very scared again. So-- and-- and the night later, or the night before, we had a message that the Ukrainian partisans came in, and they killed all the Polish people in the town-- in the village where her parents lived.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she said she wants to leave us and go to see what's happened to her parents.
